content: Compound interest is a simple but powerful way your money can grow faster over time. Instead of just earning interest on your original savings, compound interest means you earn interest on both your savings and the interest that has already been added. For example, if you save $1,000 at a 5% interest rate, after one year you’ll have $1,050. In the second year, you earn 5% not just on $1,000, but on $1,050, so your savings grow to $1,102.50. Over many years, this “interest on interest” effect causes your savings to grow much faster than with simple interest, which only pays on the original amount. After 20 years, your initial $1,000 could grow to about $2,650 with compound interest, compared to only $2,000 with simple interest. The key to maximizing compound interest is time—the longer your money stays invested, the greater the growth. Higher interest rates and adding money regularly also help, but time remains the most powerful factor.
